findings.title: No authentication mechanism configured — SecurityWebFilterChain returns 401 with no path to authenticate
findings.description: SecurityConfig correctly enforces authentication on all non-/health paths via anyExchange().authenticated(), but the chain does not configure any authentication provider, login mechanism, or JWT/OAuth2 filter. The result is a permanently locked API: unauthenticated requests to all non-health paths return 401 with no way to authenticate. This is intentional for the skeleton epic, but the absence of any auth provider creates a false sense of security — the 401 cannot be resolved by any valid credential at this stage, meaning future epics that add endpoints before wiring auth will silently be unreachable.
findings.impact: Any endpoint added in a subsequent epic before auth is wired will return 401 indefinitely. If a developer temporarily adds .permitAll() to unblock local testing and forgets to revert, an unintended open path ships to production.
findings.recommendation: Document explicitly in SecurityConfig (inline comment or JavaDoc) that no auth provider is wired until the auth epic, and that anyExchange().authenticated() is a forward-looking rule placeholder. Consider adding a TODO comment: // TODO: wire JWT/OAuth2 filter in auth epic. This prevents future developers from assuming the 401 is functional authentication rather than a stub guard.

findings.title: Security event logging set to INFO but no audit trail for 401 responses is verified
findings.description: application.properties sets logging.level.org.springframework.security=INFO, which surfaces auth events in logs. However, none of the three integration tests assert that a 401 response to /api/nonexistent produces a log entry. In production, failed auth attempts (401 on undefined paths) should be auditable. Currently, whether Spring Security actually logs the rejection at INFO level for a path that has no authentication attempt (as opposed to a failed credential check) is unverified and untested.
findings.impact: If the INFO log level does not capture unauthenticated-access rejections (as opposed to authentication failures), all 401 events on undefined paths will be silently dropped from logs, making it impossible to detect enumeration or probing attacks in production.
findings.recommendation: Add a log-assertion test or at minimum document the expected log output for the SecurityBlockTest scenario. Verify that Spring Security emits a log line (e.g., at DEBUG or INFO) when anyExchange().authenticated() rejects a request. If it does not, consider adding a custom WebFilter that logs 401 responses for audit purposes.

findings.title: CSRF disabled globally without scope restriction
findings.description: csrf(csrf -> csrf.disable()) unconditionally disables CSRF protection for all paths including /health. While this is correct for a stateless REST API using token-based auth, the disable is applied before any auth mechanism exists. If session-based authentication is accidentally configured in a future epic without re-enabling CSRF, the existing disable will silently leave all state-mutating endpoints unprotected. The current implementation gives no indication it is intentional or scoped.
findings.impact: Future developers adding session-based features without reviewing SecurityConfig will inherit CSRF-disabled state. This is a low-probability but high-impact misconfiguration risk as the codebase grows.
findings.recommendation: Add an inline comment confirming the intent: // CSRF disabled: stateless REST API — token-based auth only; do not enable session-based auth without re-enabling CSRF. This makes the security contract explicit and prevents accidental regression.

findings.title: No HTTP response security headers configured
findings.description: The SecurityWebFilterChain does not configure HTTP security headers (X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, Permissions-Policy, Content-Security-Policy). Spring Security WebFlux applies some defaults (X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-Frame-Options: DENY, Cache-Control) via HeaderWebSessionServerRequestCache, but custom headers beyond defaults are not set. For an API-only backend this is low risk, but the /health endpoint is publicly accessible and returns JSON — future endpoints may return richer content.
findings.impact: Absence of explicit header configuration means relying entirely on Spring Security defaults. If defaults change across versions, security headers may silently degrade.
findings.recommendation: Explicitly configure headers in the chain: http.headers(headers -> headers.frameOptions(fo -> fo.deny()).contentTypeOptions(Customizer.withDefaults())).csrf(...). This makes header policy explicit and version-stable. Defer Content-Security-Policy to the auth epic when the API surface is known.

findings.title: HealthEndpointTest and HealthSecurityTest are semantically duplicated
findings.description: HealthEndpointTest.healthEndpointReturns200WithOkBody and HealthSecurityTest.healthPermittedWithoutAuth execute identical HTTP calls (GET /health, no auth header, assert 200 + $.status=ok) with no observable difference in test setup. HealthSecurityTest is supposed to explicitly verify the absence of an Authorization header, but the @SpringBootTest + WebTestClient setup does not add any auth by default, so both tests are equivalent. The absence of a negative assertion (e.g., .header(HttpHeaders.AUTHORIZATION, "Bearer token") is NOT sent) means HealthSecurityTest does not add security coverage beyond HealthEndpointTest.
findings.impact: Not a direct vulnerability, but test duplication creates a false assurance that the security rule is being independently exercised. If SecurityConfig rule ordering changes, both tests will fail in the same way — one redundant test provides no additional signal.
findings.recommendation: HealthSecurityTest should be differentiated from HealthEndpointTest. Options: (1) explicitly assert via .header(HttpHeaders.AUTHORIZATION, "Bearer invalid-token") is absent; or (2) add a complementary negative test: assert that GET /health WITH an invalid Bearer token still returns 200 (confirming permitAll ignores auth headers). This makes the test semantically distinct and exercises the security rule more directly.
